bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose (London: printed for the Percy Society by T. Richards, 1844), Percy Society publication no. 48; Early English Poetry, Ballads, and Popular Literature of the Middle Ages, vol. 14.
bibliographic.publisher: Printed for the Percy Society by T. Richards
bibliographic.series: Percy Society Publications, no. 48; Early English Poetry, Ballads, and Popular Literature of the Middle Ages, vol. 14
bibliographic.title: St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose

dateStatements.label: publication
dateStatements.notes: The XML supplies the month and publication number; the inspected title pages print only M.DCCC.XLIV.

entities.description: Wright's edited juxtaposition of a Middle English verse Life transcribed from Harley MS 2277 and a prose Life taken from Wynkyn de Worde's 1527 Golden Legend, followed by seven pages of notes that selectively cite Trinity R.3.25 and Latin and French comparison texts.
entities.label: Thomas Wright, St. Brandan (1844)
notes: CME00019 encodes the title-leaf wording and printed pp. 1-56, but not Wright's preface (pp. v-viii), Percy Society council, or notes (pp. 57-63).
Wright calls the verse text a first printing, identifies Harley 2277 as copy text, and describes the Trinity manuscript only as a prior collation from which he selected two or three readings.

rights.basis: The embedded Google notice says the scanned book has entered the public domain, while warning that public-domain status varies by country.
rights.jurisdiction: Provider assessment; expressly country-variable and otherwise unspecified
notes: This records Google's assertion and qualification rather than a worldwide legal conclusion. Conditions in the same notice concern Google's scan and delivery layer, which is recorded separately.

bibliographic.citation: Jacobus de Voragine, The legende, named in Latyn Legenda aurea that is to say in Englysshe The golden legende, English translation attributed to William Caxton (London, Fleet Street at the sign of the Sun: Wynkyn de Worde, 27 August 1527), STC 24880; ESTC S111988.
bibliographic.title: The legende, named in Latyn Legenda aurea that is to say in Englysshe The golden legende

entities.description: The 27 August 1527 black-letter edition from which Wright states that he took the prose Life of Saint Brandon. In the inspected Boston Public Library copy, that Life begins partway through f. CCCXLIIIr and ends on f. CCCXLVIIr.
entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde, The Golden Legend (1527)
notes: The Boston catalog attributes the volume's English translation to William Caxton. That bibliographic attribution does not establish that Jacobus or Caxton authored or translated the added Saint Brandon component specifically.
Wright explicitly says he did not investigate the immediate source of the Lives in the English Golden Legend.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00019 XML represents a University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of the title leaf and printed pp. 1-56 of Thomas Wright's 1844 Percy Society publication St. Brandan. Wright printed two distinct English texts: an anonymous 732-line Middle English verse Life from British Library Harley MS 2277, ff. 41v-51r, with selected variants from Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.25 in the excluded notes; and a prose Life excerpted from Wynkyn de Worde's 1527 Golden Legend, where it occupies ff. CCCXLIIIr-CCCXLVIIr. The XML omits Wright's preface and pp. 57-63 notes. Complete scans of Wright's standalone publication, the enclosing Percy Society volume, and the 1527 Golden Legend are cached with exact mappings. A complete 586-canvas color IIIF bundle for Trinity R.3.25 includes covers, endleaves, adjacent works, and the Saint Brandan portion at ff. 215r-222v. Harley 2277's formerly public images are currently unavailable from the British Library, so no manuscript-image excerpt substitutes for a complete codex cache.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous English authors or adapters of two distinct textual versions
catalogSummary.date: Metrical Life composed 1250-1290; selected prose source printed 27 August 1527; edited selection published 1844
catalogSummary.region: England; no single more precise place of composition is established for both texts
catalogSummary.title: St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose

catalogingSubject.label: St. Brandan: A Medieval Legend of the Sea, in English Verse and Prose
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from Thomas Wright's 1844 Percy Society publication: its title-leaf content, the 732-line Metrical Life of St. Brandan on printed pp. 1-34, and the Prose Life of St. Brandan on pp. 35-56. The two lives are distinct textual versions with distinct immediate sources. The selection excludes Wright's half-title, Percy Society council list, preface on pp. v-viii, and notes on pp. 57-63; it is neither one medieval work nor one manuscript witness.

contentParts.label: The Metrical Life of St. Brandan
notes: Trinity R.3.25 ff. 215r-222v witnesses the poem but is omitted from the locators because Wright used it only for a few selected collations; the lineage binding records that qualified role.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English verse life in which Brendan and his monks voyage through marvelous islands and infernal and paradisal spaces before returning to Ireland.

contentParts.label: Prose Life of St. Brandan
contentParts.summary: An Early Modern English prose life of Brendan and his companions' seven-year voyage, extracted by Wright from the 1527 Golden Legend.

assertion.notes: A specialist composition range for the poem, kept distinct from the circa-1300 date and South-West English production of Harley MS 2277.

dateStatements.display: Attested by 27 August 1527 in the printed source used by Wright; earlier states are not traced in this record

scope.description: A terminus supplied by the selected printed source, not a composition date or a claim that 1527 was the prose version's first printing

extent.description: The XML has three DIV1 elements: one title-page wrapper and two content works. The metrical division has one LG and 732 L elements; the prose division has 15 P elements. Across the file there are 57 PB elements and two HEAD elements. These are encoding and edition metrics, not evidence that the two lives share authorship or an immediate source.

formStatements.description: The edition-level selection juxtaposes one verse life and one prose life.
A 732-line poem in septenary rhymed couplets.
A prose saint's life extracted from the 1527 Golden Legend.

assertion.notes: The top-level XML LANG='enm' is too broad for this prose part; its 1527 printed language is modeled separately.

scope.description: Edition title, headings, and retained editorial presentation

notes: The preferred title describes Wright's 1844 edited selection, not a single medieval work title.
The metrical and prose lives are cataloged as distinct content parts with independent language, form, date, and source statements.

Harley MS 2277 is the metrical base witness; Trinity R.3.25 is only a selectively collated witness. OCR readings R.3.26 and R.8.25 are rejected after direct inspection of Wright's preface and the Trinity catalogs.
The 1527 Golden Legend is an immediate printed source for Wright's prose selection, not a manuscript witness and not proof that the underlying prose version originated in 1527.
The record remains partial because the inter-version source relationship, prose version history, title-leaf discrepancy, and direct base-witness verification remain unresolved.

next_steps: Consult current specialist scholarship on the South English Legendary Brendan and the English Golden Legend tradition.
Collate representative narrative units without assuming that broad plot agreement proves direct dependence.
Keep the two parts modeled as distinct works until a specific source route is evidenced.
notes: Wright explicitly declined to determine which English version represented the original more faithfully.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact textual relationship and direction of dependence between the metrical and prose English versions, and what are their respective relationships to the wider Brendan tradition?

next_steps: Trace the prose text through earlier Caxton and de Worde Golden Legend editions.
Consult bibliographical and textual scholarship on the English Golden Legend translation and revision history.
Keep 1527 scoped to Wright's printed source rather than treating it as a composition date.
openQuestions.question: When and by whom was the particular English prose Brendan version printed in the 1527 Golden Legend first composed or translated?

assertion.notes: The two texts belong to English-language traditions, but the evidence does not establish one precise place of composition for the selection.
Neither selected English text has a securely identified author in the evidence inspected.

scope.description: The two selected textual versions collectively, without implying a single author
The juxtaposition of metrical and prose versions in the 1844 publication
The metrical text printed from British Library Harley MS 2277

assertion.notes: Wright describes an earlier collation and prints only two or three selected readings; R.3.25 is not modeled as a coequal base text.

scope.description: Selected readings from Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.25

subjects.label: Terrestrial Paradise and the Land of Promise

summaries.value: Two English versions recount Brendan's seven-year voyage with a company of monks through a sacred and marvelous ocean landscape. They encounter providential islands and communities, the whale Jasconius, birds identified with fallen angels, demons and Judas, hermits, and finally the Land of Promise before Brendan returns to Ireland and dies.
The XML contains a title-page division followed by two complete content divisions: the 732-line Metrical Life on printed pp. 1-34 and the Prose Life in fifteen encoded paragraphs on pp. 35-56.

The record describes the encoded edition-level selection. Wright printed the verse from Harley MS 2277 and cited a limited earlier collation against Trinity R.3.25; he took the prose text from Wynkyn de Worde's 1527 Golden Legend. Preface and notes are evidence for the record but are outside the encoded selection.

Textual, physical, editorial, and digital layers are kept separate. Harley 2277 is the verse base witness; R.3.25 is a selectively collated witness; the Golden Legend is the prose source edition; Wright's publication is the immediate edition; U-M is a digital encoding; and the checked-in XML is the repository artifact. The South-Western dialect and circa-1300 date of Harley 2277 are not assigned to the entire edited selection.

assertion.notes: The title is the edition-level wrapper for the two selected lives, not a shared medieval title.
